findings.title: Single-flight lock may guard a race unreachable under current dev server
findings.why_it_matters: The new `_bulk_publish_lock` (threading.Lock, non-blocking acquire) is designed to reject a second concurrent bulk-publish-now call with 409 while the first is mid-flight. `webui.py`'s `app.run(host=bind_host, port=port, debug=debug_mode, use_reloader=False)` does not pass `threaded=True`, so Werkzeug's dev server defaults to a single-threaded, sequential request loop (no ThreadingMixIn). Under that configuration a second HTTP request literally cannot enter the Flask app while the first `bulk-publish-now` request (which runs its whole loop synchronously, no background thread) is still executing -- it would queue at the socket/accept level and only be dispatched after the first response is sent, by which point the lock is already released. If that is indeed the only way this app is served, the lock + 409 branch + accompanying test are defensive code for a scenario that cannot occur in this deployment, adding real complexity (a module-level lock, try/finally, an extra error_class, and a dedicated recursive test) for a condition that only matters if the app is later served under a threaded/multi-worker WSGI setup. This isn't wrong -- `threading.Lock()` with a non-blocking acquire is the correct primitive if concurrency ever does happen (it correctly mirrors the codebase's existing lock idiom in keepalive_job.py/_keepalive_engine.py/url_verify_throttle.py) -- but the team should confirm whether concurrent access is actually possible in the intended deployment, since if it isn't, the extra surface is unexercised in practice and its only real test coverage is a same-thread recursive simulation.
findings.suggested_fix: Confirm (or document) whether the WebUI is ever served with threaded=True / multiple workers. If it is always single-threaded (as webui.py's app.run() call currently is), consider noting that explicitly in the single-flight guard's docstring as forward-looking defense-in-depth rather than implying it protects an active race today; if multi-threaded serving is planned, this is exactly the right primitive to have in place already.
evidence: webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py:35 defines `_bulk_publish_lock = threading.Lock()` with acquire(blocking=False) at line 146 guarding drafts_bulk_publish_now, which runs its entire ids loop synchronously in the request thread (no background thread spawned), unlike webui_app/services/keepalive_job.py's start_recheck() which only locks a quick check-and-set before handing work to a spawned threading.Thread.

webui.py line ~112: `app.run(host=bind_host, port=port, debug=debug_mode, use_reloader=False)` -- no `threaded=True`/`processes=` argument, so Werkzeug's run_simple defaults to a non-threaded, single-process synchronous WSGIServer that serves one request at a time.

tests/test_webui_api_v1_bulk_ops.py's test_webui_drafts_bulk_publish_now_double_submit_returns_409 simulates the 'concurrent' second request via a *recursive* client.post() call made from inside the mocked bulk_publish_now, i.e. same-thread re-entrancy rather than a real concurrent-thread test, which is consistent with -- and does not disprove -- the single-threaded-server reading.

residual_risks: The `_bulk_publish_lock` only guards `/drafts/bulk-publish-now`; sibling mutating endpoints on the same resources (`/drafts/bulk-cancel`, `/drafts/bulk-delete`, single-item `/drafts/publish-now|cancel|delete`) are not covered by any lock, so if the server is ever run multi-threaded, races between an in-flight bulk-publish-now and a concurrent bulk-cancel/bulk-delete touching the same draft ids and APScheduler jobs remain possible -- the new guard only prevents same-endpoint double-submission, not cross-endpoint interleavings.

`_require_ids` in webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py is now a verbatim duplicate of the pre-existing helper in webui_app/api/v1/history.py (mirroring the already-duplicated `_require_id`). Acceptable at 2 call sites given the established per-module-helper convention, but a third bulk-ids endpoint module would tip this toward warranting a small shared validation helper instead of a third copy.

`_INFRA_FAILURE_CODES` is a mutable `set` literal used only for read-only membership checks; a `frozenset` would better signal it's a fixed constant, though this has no functional consequence since nothing in the diff mutates it.
testing_gaps: No test exercises the case where `_api.bulk_publish_now(ids)` raises an unhandled exception (as opposed to returning a controlled `{"ok": False, ...}` dict) inside `drafts_bulk_publish_now` -- the `try/finally` around `_bulk_publish_lock` should release the lock correctly in that path, but it is currently unverified by any test, and a regression there (e.g. an accidental early `return` bypassing the `finally`) would silently wedge the endpoint into permanent 409s until process restart.

The double-submit test (test_webui_drafts_bulk_publish_now_double_submit_returns_409) only proves the lock is held during the first call and rejects a same-thread reentrant call; there is no follow-up assertion that a *subsequent* call after the first completes succeeds (i.e. that the lock is fully released and reusable), though this is implied by the `finally` block.

webui_app/api/v1/spec.py's three new `spec.path(...)` blocks are only indirectly verified via tests/test_webui_api_v1.py's committed-YAML staleness check (not in the reviewed test file), so a reviewer scanning only test_webui_api_v1_bulk_ops.py would not see direct evidence the OpenAPI additions are exercised.
